title: OpenProject vs Teamwork for Power users
meta_description: Compare OpenProject vs Teamwork for power users. See which project system gives full infrastructure control, hosting freedom, and deeper customization at scale.
constraint_lens: You need the project system to stay under your infrastructure and admin control as requirements get more demanding.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for power users who need the project system on infrastructure they control.
one_second_verdict.reason: Teamwork fails first because you cannot run the system on infrastructure you control.

verdict.summary: OpenProject is the better choice when control of the platform is part of the requirement, not a side detail. The decision boundary is whether the team can live inside a vendor-hosted workspace or needs to choose where the system runs, when it upgrades, and how it is configured. Teamwork remains easier if nobody wants to own server administration.
verdict.decision_rule: If the project management system cannot be self-hosted and modified at the infrastructure level, Teamwork fails first.

sections.heading: When platform control is part of the requirement
sections.content: This user is evaluating the platform as well as the feature set. They care about where the system runs, who can administer it, and whether upgrades happen on their terms. OpenProject fits because infrastructure control is part of the product rather than an afterthought.

bullets.point: The system can live on infrastructure you control
bullets.why_it_matters: OpenProject fits teams that need admin access, internal hosting, or tighter control over how the project platform is run.

bullets.point: Work is tracked as structured issues, not generic tasks
bullets.why_it_matters: OpenProject makes filtering, triage, and ownership easier because each record carries the fields needed for real engineering or product work.

bullets.point: Each task keeps a technical audit trail
bullets.why_it_matters: OpenProject preserves comments, state changes, and code references on one record, which helps when work needs to be traced later.

bullets.point: The platform work is handled for you
bullets.why_it_matters: Teamwork gives the team a vendor-run workspace with less setup and fewer admin chores, which is useful when nobody wants to manage the platform itself.

bullets.point: Status is easy to scan on a visual board
bullets.why_it_matters: Teamwork makes it obvious what is waiting, moving, or done without opening a reporting view or managing extra structure.

bullets.point: Comments and files stay attached to the task
bullets.why_it_matters: Teamwork keeps lightweight collaboration on the work item itself, which is helpful when the team mainly needs a shared task surface.

items.fails_when: No one wants to run servers, manage upgrades, or own the environment behind the tool.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose Teamwork if the team wants the platform managed for them and does not need infrastructure control.

items.fails_when: The team needs to decide where the system runs, how it is configured, or when it is upgraded and the hosted service does not allow that.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose OpenProject when infrastructure control is part of the requirement, not a nice-to-have.

sections.heading: When the loser can still make sense
sections.content: This can flip if nobody on the team wants platform ownership and fast onboarding matters more than infrastructure control. In that case, Teamwork can be the saner choice.

sections.heading: Quick rules
rules: Choose OpenProject if the platform must run on infrastructure you control.
Choose Teamwork if the team wants the software managed for them.
Avoid Teamwork when admin access and upgrade timing are part of the requirement.

faqs.q: Why does self-hosting change the verdict here?
faqs.a: Because the decision is not only about features. It is also about who controls the server, configuration, and upgrade timing behind the project tool.

faqs.q: What is the downside of choosing OpenProject?
faqs.a: OpenProject asks the team to own more of the platform itself, which is a real cost if nobody wants that responsibility.

faqs.q: When is Teamwork the better choice?
faqs.a: Teamwork is better when the team wants fast onboarding and a managed service more than infrastructure control.

faqs.q: Who usually benefits most from OpenProject?
faqs.a: Teams with hosting requirements, admin control needs, or internal policies that make vendor-only deployment a poor fit.
